ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dal Makhani. FOODON_03311737 (lentil dish) is the closest parent category. Tandoori roti component has no separate FoodOn term; the combined dish is represented under the lentil dish parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Dal Makhani. A00LC (legume-based dish) is the closest defensible parent. The tandoori roti component is not separately coded; the combined meal is anchored under legume-based dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Dal Makhani or tandoori roti as a combined dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food preparation codes at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Dal Makhani with Tandoori Roti consists of approximately 250 g of dal makhani curry and two tandoori roti (approximately 65 g each), totalling around 380 g.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Makhani (ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, composite estimate) and Tandoori Roti (ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, whole wheat chapati / tandoor roti entry)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Dal Makhani nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN 2017 composite data for cooked whole black lentil (urad dal) preparations with butter and cream, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lentils (NDB 16069) and cream/butter additions. Tandoori roti values are from ICMR-NIN 2017 whole wheat chapati/roti entry. The combined dish per-100g values are a weighted blend: approximately 66% dal makhani and 34% tandoori roti by weight, reflecting the standard 250g dal + 130g roti serving. Values are consistent with published Indian dietary analysis literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of lentils and legume-based Indian dishes; whole wheat roti GI data — University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dal Makhani GI is estimated as a weighted composite: whole cooked black lentils (urad dal) have a GI of approximately 30–38 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283); whole wheat tandoori roti has a GI of approximately 62 (University of Sydney GI Database, chapati/whole wheat flatbread entry). Weighted blend (66% dal, 34% roti by weight) yields a composite GI of approximately 37, categorised as low. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (37 × 70.3) / 100 ≈ 26.0 g (carbohydrate per 380g serving ≈ 70.3g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100g; ~1178 mg per full serving)
hypertension.guidance: The sodium content of a full restaurant serving of Dal Makhani with Tandoori Roti (~1178 mg) approaches or exceeds 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low composite glycemic index (~37) driven by high-fibre whole black lentils
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~37) and high dietary fibre content (~3.4 g/100g) of Dal Makhani with Tandoori Roti support gradual postprandial glucose release, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; the roti component contributes the majority of the glycemic load and should be limited to one piece for tighter glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and cream (~3.1 g/100g); beneficial legume fibre and plant protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the legume base of Dal Makhani provides cardioprotective dietary fibre and plant protein, the butter and cream used in restaurant preparations contribute saturated fat (~3.1 g/100g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-butter versions and limit portion size to manage saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Makhani with Tandoori Roti is a classic North Indian Punjabi meal combination consisting of two components: Dal Makhani, a rich slow-cooked curry made from whole black lentils (urad dal) and red kidney beans (rajma) simmered overnight with butter, cream, tomatoes, onion, garlic, ginger, and aromatic spices including cumin and garam masala; and Tandoori Roti, an unleavened whole wheat flatbread baked directly on the inner wall of a clay tandoor oven at high heat, giving it a slightly charred, smoky exterior and soft interior. Dal Makhani is distinguished by its velvety, buttery texture and deep umami flavour developed through prolonged slow cooking, traditionally over a wood fire or low flame for 6–8 hours. The dish is a staple of Punjabi home cooking and is widely served in Indian restaurants globally. Nutritionally, the combination provides a balanced macronutrient profile: the lentil base contributes substantial plant protein (~6.2 g/100g), dietary fibre (~3.4 g/100g), iron, folate, and potassium, while the roti adds complex carbohydrates from whole wheat. The composite glycemic index is low (~37), making this meal suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical concern is sodium (~310 mg/100g) from salt and spice blends used in restaurant preparation, and saturated fat from butter and cream. This dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable. It is commonly served as a main course at lunch or dinner and is a popular choice at Indian vegetarian restaurants in Singapore, including establishments at Boat Quay.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 Indian Food Composition Tables using a weighted composite of dal makhani (cooked whole black lentil preparation with butter and cream) and whole wheat tandoori roti entries. No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for the exact combined dish as served at this restaurant. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for whole black lentils and whole wheat chapati. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact restaurant recipe (butter and cream quantities, salt levels) is unknown and values represent a reasonable central estimate.
notes: 1. The food_name in the input ('Daal Makhni❤️ Free Tandoori Roti') contains a heart emoji and the word 'Free', which appears to be a menu promotional label ('complimentary tandoori roti included with dal makhani order') rather than a separate food item. The canonical food name has been normalised to 'Dal Makhani with Tandoori Roti'. 2. Nutrient values represent a weighted per-100g composite of approximately 66% dal makhani and 34% tandoori roti by weight, based on a standard restaurant serving of 250g dal + 2 roti (~65g each). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for this food category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for this food category. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dal Makhani with Tandoori Roti as a combined dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The composite GI of ~37 is derived from weighted GI values for whole black lentils (~34, low GI) and whole wheat chapati (~62, medium GI). 6. Sodium estimate (~310 mg/100g) reflects typical restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may have lower sodium. 7. The dish is served at Jewel of India, Boat Quay, Singapore (GrabFood listing). 8. FoodOn match type is closest_parent as no exact FoodOn term for Dal Makhani exists. FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent for the same reason.
